adjudicated.title: reclaimSquatter demotes the victim to Pending but only closes the one conflicting lease, stranding the victim's other-group open leases on a non-terminal run

votes.reasoning:
The finding's mechanical claim is TRUE and I confirmed it by execution: reclaimSquatter closes only the one conflicting lease (group-0), demotes the victim to Pending, and group-1's lease stays open across 20 reconciles / 20 simulated hours on a non-terminal run, so INV-TERMINAL-PRESENT (which gates on r.Terminal) never fires. BUT the state is LEGAL, not a leak, on four independent grounds:

1) The oracle — the repo's own authority on legality, which panics under `go test` — did NOT fire on ANY of the 20 reconciles. A Pending run holding open leases is exactly the half-assembled-gang state the engine is built to produce. invariant.go's package doc explicitly REJECTS the neighbouring invariant "a Running run holds at least one open active lease" as false-in-legal-states; the width invariant gates on Phase==Running, and the terminal invariant on Terminal. Pending-holding-open is deliberately uncovered.

2) The run does not wedge: run_controller.go's adoption path (case allocated>0) deliberately parks the run Pending, re-emits the missing slice via topUpActiveGang (my test observed 4 victim pods re-emitted), and — proven by my second test (F1b) — the moment a replacement group-0 lease is minted the run adopts back to Running and REUSES the still-open group-1 lease (phase=Running, g1Closed=false). "Stranded" is the wrong word; the lease is adopted and reused.

3) reclaimSquatter runs ONLY for an Unfunded victim — its sole caller is guarded by `class == funding.ClassUnfunded`. An unfunded open lease charges no budget (it runs on capacity nobody paid for), so the immortal-lease harm model ("charges a budget forever") does not apply. The GPUs it holds are reclaimable by any funded admission (reclaimForAdmission / the resolver), so it is not a permanent capacity hold either. This is the documented unfunded contract (quota-semantics R14: "it requeues and re-admits when capacity returns"), which reclaimSquatter's own comment states.

4) The immortal-lease class is specifically a lease on a CORPSE that nothing reconciles. This victim is Pending and reconciled every tick, not terminal. The only path to terminal is failRun, which closes every lease (closeRunLeases) — so there is no reachable state where the victim is terminal AND holds group-1 open. The deliberate absence of a CheckpointDeadline (unlike failGroupWithoutSpare) is correct: unfunded work is not entitled to a bounded grace that fails-and-releases; it is entitled to wait indefinitely for opportunistic capacity.

On the reachability question the task raised: b4f6a6d's runPhaseTracker changes only WHICH phase wins when multiple writers race for one run's Status.Phase; it neither adds nor removes a lease-closing step, so it does not open or close F1. The demotion to Pending and the open group-1 lease are unchanged by it. The prior panel's "refuted as pre-existing" is also correct as a matter of fact — group-1's lease was already open (the run was Running over 4 GPU) before the node failure; reclaimSquatter did not MINT a new lease, it reduced the victim's footprint and demoted it — but pre-existence is not why I rule not-a-defect; the legality (oracle-clean, re-admits/reuses, unfunded+reclaimable, non-terminal) is.
votes.reproduction: Copied the repo to a temp dir at HEAD e2b5355 and added controllers/f1_probe_test.go. Scenario: node-a and node-b (4 GPU each). swap run (funded, budget "team") holds active on node-a#0,#1 and spare on node-b#0,#1. victim run (owner org:ai:nobody, budget "", fixed-width 4 GPU) holds group-0 on node-b#0,#1 (the spare's exact slots) and group-1 on node-b#2,#3. Ran HandleNodeFailure("node-a"), then Reconcile("default","victim") 20 times advancing the clock 1h each. Command: `go test ./controllers/ -run TestF1_ -v`. Result (exit 0, no oracle panic): after HandleNodeFailure victim.Phase=Pending, victim-g0 closed reason "ReclaimedBySpare", victim-g1 OPEN, CheckpointDeadline=nil. After 20 reconciles/20h: victim.Phase=Pending "assembling gang: 2/4 GPUs held", victim-g1 still open, 1 open victim lease, 4 victim pods re-emitted. Second test F1b: after minting a replacement group-0 lease and one reconcile, victim.Phase=Running with group-1 NOT closed (reused) — the run re-admits and reuses the lease.
votes.recommendedFix: None required. If the team nonetheless wants an unfunded fixed-width run that provably can never reassemble to stop occupying slots, the correct place is NOT reclaimSquatter (killing it would violate the quota-semantics R14 unfunded-requeue contract and the "start together or not at all" assembly design); it would be a separate, funding-class-aware GC of long-idle assembling runs. But note that group-1 here is unfunded and already reclaimable by any funded admission, so there is no capacity or budget leak to fix.

votes.reasoning:
F1's factual observations are all TRUE, but the end state it flags is a legal, documented state, not a leak. I copied the tree at HEAD (e2b5355) and drove the exact scenario as a pure-engine simulation.

WHAT IS TRUE (TestF1_StrandedOtherGroupLease): a fixed-width 4-GPU unfunded run holding group-0 on the spare's exact slots and group-1 on node-c is demoted to Pending by reclaimSquatter, which closes only the passed lease (run_controller.go:1477 `CloseLease(lease, "ReclaimedBySpare", now)`) and leaves filler-g1 open. Driven through 20 Reconciles over 20 simulated hours the run stays `Pending`, message "assembling gang: 2/4 GPUs held", and filler-g1 never closes. The oracle never panics — INV-TERMINAL-PRESENT only constrains terminal runs (invariant.go:187 `if r.Terminal {`), and Pending is not terminal.

WHY IT IS LEGAL, NOT A LEAK. (1) The demoted run does not wedge: it lands in the adoption path's half-assembled-gang branch, which DELIBERATELY parks a run Pending while it holds open leases and re-emits the missing ranks (run_controller.go:239-240, topUpActiveGang). (2) TestF1_FreshHalfAssembledIsLegal shows a FRESH unfunded run that only ever minted 2 of its 4 GPUs produces the byte-identical state — Pending, holding one open lease, same "assembling gang: 2/4" message, oracle silent. The state F1 flags is therefore indistinguishable from the ordinary partially-placed gang; the oracle accepts both. (3) TestF1_ReAdoptsReusingHeldLease shows that when the missing rank is re-minted (as the plugin does when capacity returns) the run adopts back to Running REUSING the held lease (it stays open, is not re-minted) — "adopted 4 GPUs of open leases from an earlier admission". This is the "demote, do not kill; requeues and re-admits when capacity returns" behavior the reclaimSquatter comment states (run_controller.go:1452-1453).

CONSEQUENCE IF ASSUMED REAL. The stranded lease is UNFUNDED by construction — reclaimSquatter is reached only for `class == funding.ClassUnfunded` (run_controller.go:1298). An unfunded lease charges NO budget (it runs on capacity nobody paid for), so nothing is billed forever. It holds 2 GPUs, but unfunded work is reclaimable by definition: any funded admission with a deficit takes it via reclaimForAdmission/applyResolution (OnlyUnfunded). So there is no permanent budget charge and no permanent capacity lock — only opportunistic capacity held while the run waits to reassemble, surrendered the instant funded work needs it.

REAPER CHECK. A "fix" that closed the victim's other-group leases on demotion, or an invariant "a Pending run holds no open lease", would reap the legal half-assembled-gang state the adoption path routinely produces (line 239 sets Pending while `allocated > 0`), forcing every requeued unfunded run and every partially-placed gang to discard acquired ranks and re-acquire from scratch. That is exactly the rejected-invariant class documented in pkg/invariant. b4f6a6d's routing of reclaimSquatter's phase through runPhaseTracker only changes which verdict wins when multiple writers touch one run in a pass; it does not change reachability of this state, and the state remains legal.
votes.reproduction: Copied HEAD (e2b5355) to a temp dir and added controllers/f1_probe_test.go, then ran `go test ./controllers/ -run TestF1_ -v`. TestF1_StrandedOtherGroupLease: HandleNodeFailure("node-a") demotes filler to Pending, closes filler-g0 (ReclaimedBySpare), leaves filler-g1 open; 20 Reconciles / 20h keep it Pending "assembling gang: 2/4 GPUs held" with filler-g1 open — oracle never panics. TestF1_FreshHalfAssembledIsLegal: a fresh unfunded run with only 2/4 GPUs minted produces the identical Pending+open-lease state, oracle silent. TestF1_ReAdoptsReusingHeldLease: re-minting the missing rank flips the run to Running reusing the held lease (still open). All three pass.
votes.recommendedFix: None. The flagged state is legal (the documented half-assembled-gang / requeued-unfunded park). A fix that closed the held lease or added a "Pending holds no open lease" invariant would be a reaper against the adoption path at run_controller.go:239 and every partially-placed gang. If observability is desired, the only defensible change is non-behavioral: emit a periodic event on a long-Pending unfunded run so operators can see it is waiting to reassemble — but do not close its lease and do not fail it, since unfunded work is meant to requeue indefinitely (R14).

adjudicated.title: reclaimSquatter skips pod removal entirely when the squatter lease has an empty group label, leaving the victim's pod on the reclaimed slots

votes.reasoning:
F2 was a REAL defect in the reviewed commit 98b602d and is CLOSED by commit 8a1dc2c (line `group := leaseGroupIndex(lease)` at controllers/run_controller.go:1476, together with deletion of the `if group != ""` guard). I verified the fix by execution and mutation, not by reading.

TRACE OF THE ORIGINAL (98b602d). `git show 98b602d:controllers/run_controller.go` line 1456 reads `group := lease.Labels[binder.LabelGroupIndex]` and line 1465 guards `if group != "" { c.removePodsForGroups(victimKey, map[string]struct{}{group: {}}) }`. The sole production mint path (admission.PodLeaseWithRole) stamps no LabelGroupIndex, so the raw read returns "", the guard is false, and pod removal is skipped entirely — the lease is closed (ledger frees the slot) while the container keeps running on the exact node#ordinal the swap targets. That is exactly F2.

TRACE OF HEAD (e2b5355). reclaimSquatter now does `group := leaseGroupIndex(lease)` (run_controller.go:1476); leaseGroupIndex (2128-2135) returns "0" when the label is absent/empty. The `if group != ""` guard is gone, so `c.removePodsForGroups(victimKey, map[string]struct{}{group: {}})` (1485) runs unconditionally with group="0". removePodsForGroups (2639-2655) reads each pod's raw LabelGroupIndex; emitCohortPods/emitSparePods hardcode "0", so pod-"0" is in the {"0"} set and the victim pod is dropped from c.State.Pods. Lease-side and pod-side now agree at "0", so the pod is really removed.

EXECUTION. Copied the repo to /tmp/f2work and ran TestReclaimedSquatterIsEvictedInBothPlanesWithAProductionShapedLease (drives the real HandleNodeFailure with prodLease(), which deletes LabelGroupIndex to match what PodLeaseWithRole mints): PASS. MUTATION: reverting line 1476 to `group := lease.Labels[binder.LabelGroupIndex]` (the 98b602d read) makes the production-shaped test FAIL with F2's exact message — "the squatter's container filler-active-0 still holds the slots the swap targets. The lease says group \"<absent>\" and the pod says \"0\"" — while the older fixture-rich TestReclaimedSquatterIsEvictedInBothPlanes still PASSES. This proves (a) F2 was a true under-eviction defect and (b) the fix is LOAD-BEARING ON THE PRODUCTION PATH, not dead code like F3: the production-shaped fixture carries no group label, exactly as the real system mints.

HARDER QUESTIONS. (1) Is defaulting to "0" right today? Yes, and only because the world is uniformly "0": every lease lacks the label (→"0") and every pod hardcodes "0". They agree by construction. (2) Could the default evict the wrong group once R28b lands and labels become real? That is a FUTURE hazard, not a current defect. The author documents it explicitly in the 8a1dc2c message and R28-group-identity.md: "The '0' default then becomes a migration shim and must be DELETED — a permanent silent default would hide the next instance of this bug." A related latent issue — reclaiming ONE squatter lease calls removePodsForGroups({"0"}), which removes ALL of a multi-lease victim run's pods while closing only the one lease — is the same R28b no-per-group-identity limitation (documented, open), and it is over-eviction, the opposite direction from F2's claim. Neither is what F2 alleges, and neither is a current correctness break because there is exactly one group. So F2 itself is fixed; the reaper it points at is filed as R28b and is out of F2's scope.
votes.reproduction: In /tmp/f2work (copy of repo at HEAD e2b5355): `go test ./controllers/ -run TestReclaimedSquatterIsEvictedInBothPlanes -count=1 -v` -> both PASS. Then edit controllers/run_controller.go line 1476 from `group := leaseGroupIndex(lease)` to `group := lease.Labels[binder.LabelGroupIndex]` and re-run -> TestReclaimedSquatterIsEvictedInBothPlanesWithAProductionShapedLease FAILS at resolver_settlement_test.go:341 ("the squatter's container filler-active-0 still holds the slots the swap targets"), the fixture-rich TestReclaimedSquatterIsEvictedInBothPlanes still PASSES. This is F2 reproduced against the reverted fix and confirmed absent on HEAD.
votes.recommendedFix: No fix needed for F2: it is closed on HEAD. Carry the residual reaper forward under R28b: when the packer's real group indices reach both leases (plugin stamps the label from the pod, fail-closed PreBind gate on the R5/R6 model) and pods, the "0" default in leaseGroupIndex (run_controller.go:2128 and the twin in pkg/resolver/resolver.go:271) MUST be deleted so a label-less lease no longer silently collapses into group "0"; otherwise reclaimSquatter could match the wrong group's pods. Also, once groups are real, reclaimSquatter's removePodsForGroups({group}) should remove only the reclaimed group's pods rather than every pod sharing the collapsed "0", so a multi-lease victim's untouched groups are not evicted.

adjudicated.title: reclaimSquatter's pod eviction is dead code in production: plugin-minted leases carry no group-index label

votes.reasoning:
F3 claimed reclaimSquatter's pod eviction was dead code in production: it keyed pod removal on a group-index label that the sole committer (admission.PodLeaseWithRole) never stamps, so in a real cluster the lease read returns "" while emitted pods carry "0", so removePodsForGroups matched nothing — a ledger-only eviction. The author claims 8a1dc2c fixed it. I verified the fix adversarially by CODE TRACE and by EXECUTION.

CODE TRACE (HEAD=e2b5355): reclaimSquatter (controllers/run_controller.go:1469) now computes `group := leaseGroupIndex(lease)` (line 1476), then `c.removePodsForGroups(victimKey, map[string]struct{}{group: {}})` (line 1485). leaseGroupIndex (line 2128) returns the label only when present AND non-empty (`ok && idx != ""`, line 2130), otherwise returns "0" (line 2134). removePodsForGroups (line 2639) removes pods whose raw pod label `pod.Labels[binder.LabelGroupIndex]` (line 2646) is in the set. For a production-shaped lease with no group label, group resolves to "0", which matches the pods emitCohortPods/emitSparePods hardcode as "0". So the pod-plane eviction now fires.

EXECUTION: I copied the tree via `git archive HEAD`, built clean, and ran the two eviction tests — both PASS, including TestReclaimedSquatterIsEvictedInBothPlanesWithAProductionShapedLease, which drives the real HandleNodeFailure with prodLease() (LabelRunName + LabelRunRole, group label deleted — exactly PodLeaseWithRole's shape). MUTATION M9: I reverted reclaimSquatter to the raw read `group := lease.Labels[binder.LabelGroupIndex]`. The production-shaped test then FAILED ("the squatter's container filler-active-0 still holds the slots... The lease says group \"<absent>\" and the pod says \"0\""), while the older fixture-rich test (which sets the label) still PASSED. This proves the fix is load-bearing and not decorative, and that it evicts pods for a lease shaped exactly like the sole committer's output. A fix that were inert in production would have kept the production-shaped test passing under the mutation; it did not.

OTHER CONSUMERS: grep for raw `Labels[binder.LabelGroupIndex]` across production code (excluding tests/worktrees) returns only lines 1680 and 2646 (both POD-side, correct — pods really carry "0"), and the two helper bodies (controller line 2130, resolver line 273). No stray raw lease-side read remains. CLONED-OBLIGATION (playbook class 7): pkg/resolver/resolver.go:271 is a SECOND implementation of leaseGroupIndex that differs subtly — it returns idx whenever the key is present (`ok`, line 273), so a present-but-empty label yields "" there but "0" in the controller. In production this divergence is latent, not live: PodLeaseWithRole never stamps the label so `ok` is false in both and both return "0"; binder.buildLease (the only path that stamps a value, always numeric) is dead post-cutover. So the resolver's pod eviction (applyResolution, action.GroupIndex="0" matched against pod label "0") also works today. The cloned helper is a real class-7 smell worth consolidating but does not reopen F3.

Conclusion: the reclaimSquatter dead-code defect that F3 describes is genuinely closed by 8a1dc2c, verified by execution and mutation, not merely by inspection. The deeper R28b problem (packer groups never reach leases/pods so every lease is uniformly "0") remains open by the author's own admission, but that is a separate, filed finding.
votes.reproduction: Copied HEAD (e2b5355) via `git archive HEAD | tar -x` into a temp dir; `go build ./...` clean. Ran `go test ./controllers/ -run TestReclaimedSquatterIsEvictedInBothPlanes -v`: both TestReclaimedSquatterIsEvictedInBothPlanesWithAProductionShapedLease and TestReclaimedSquatterIsEvictedInBothPlanes PASS. Mutation M9: replaced `group := leaseGroupIndex(lease)` with `group := lease.Labels[binder.LabelGroupIndex]` in reclaimSquatter; rebuilt; the production-shaped test FAILED (squatter pod filler-active-0 not evicted) while the fixture-rich test still PASSED — confirming the fix is load-bearing and evicts pods for a lease shaped exactly like admission.PodLeaseWithRole's output.
votes.recommendedFix: No fix needed for F3 itself — closed by 8a1dc2c and verified by execution/mutation. Secondary cleanup (class-7 cloned obligation): consolidate the two leaseGroupIndex implementations (controllers/run_controller.go:2128 and pkg/resolver/resolver.go:271) into one, and align the empty-string handling (resolver returns "" for a present-but-empty label; controller defaults it to "0"). Latent today because the sole committer never stamps the label, but it will diverge the moment R28b makes the label real, exactly the kind of silent default the author warns must be deleted, not left in two subtly different copies.

adjudicated.title: reclaimSquatter closes one lease but deletes the whole group's pods, over-evicting and orphaning sibling leases

votes.reasoning:
F4 is a real defect in the current tree (HEAD e2b5355), and I confirmed it by execution rather than inspection.

The path: HandleNodeFailure pass 2 detects conflicts at SLOT granularity — line 1277 skips any lease that does not occupy the spare's exact node#ordinal slots (`!leaseOccupiesSlots(other, spareSlots)`; leaseOccupiesSlots returns true if the lease holds ANY of the exact slots). For each unfunded conflicting lease it calls `c.reclaimSquatter(other, ...)` (line 1303). reclaimSquatter closes only that ONE lease (`CloseLease(lease, "ReclaimedBySpare", now)`, line 1477) but then evicts pods at GROUP granularity: `c.removePodsForGroups(victimKey, map[string]struct{}{group: {}})` (line 1485), deleting every pod of the victim whose group label matches. The two granularities do not agree, and reclaimSquatter never closes the victim's other open leases.

Because the sole committer stamps no group index every lease reads group "0" (leaseGroupIndex defaults to "0"), and every controller-emitted pod is hardcoded "0" (R28b). So removePodsForGroups({"0"}) deletes ALL of the victim's pods, while the caller loop closes only the leases whose slots intersect the spare set. Any victim wider than the spare — the normal case, since the sole committer mints one lease per pod, so a multi-rank unfunded run has multiple leases — keeps open leases on non-spare slots whose pods were just deleted. An OPEN lease charges a budget and holds GPUs (CORE FACT), so the run pays forever for GPUs whose containers are gone, and containers on slots the swap never needed are killed (over-eviction).

I proved it: a victim ("filler", unfunded) holding two leases, filler-b0 on node-b#0 (the spare's slot) and filler-b1 on node-b#1 (not), with two group-"0" pods. After HandleNodeFailure("node-a"): filler-b0 closed=ReclaimedBySpare, filler-b1 still OPEN, and BOTH pods deleted. Over-eviction and orphaned lease, exactly as F4 describes.

This is not merely pre-existing: commit 8a1dc2c ACTIVATED it. In 98b602d reclaimSquatter read the raw label (`group := lease.Labels[binder.LabelGroupIndex]`, i.e. "") and guarded `if group != ""`, so removePodsForGroups was never called — the reclaim was inert in the pod plane. 8a1dc2c changed it to `group := leaseGroupIndex(lease)` (defaults "0") and DELETED the `if group != ""` guard, so pod eviction now fires at group granularity while still closing a single lease. So 8a1dc2c turned an inert reclaim into a working-but-over-evicting one and did not close the sibling leases. No later commit (b4f6a6d, e2b5355) touches this.

The shipped test TestReclaimedSquatterIsEvictedInBothPlanesWithAProductionShapedLease hides it because its victim has ONE lease covering exactly the spare's slots and ONE pod — closing that lease frees precisely the pods deleted, so no orphan surfaces.

Direction of fix: option (a). The pod model (PodManifest carries NodeName+GPUs, not ordinals) makes slot-granular pod removal impossible, so group-granular pod eviction is forced; the remedy is to make the LEASES follow the pods. I implemented it (close every open lease of the victim matching the same leaseGroupIndex predicate used for pod deletion) in a temp copy: the F4 test then passes (filler-b1 closed too) and the full controllers suite still passes. This is not a reaper: reclaimSquatter is only reached for ClassUnfunded victims (call-site guard at line 1298), it only touches the victim's own leases, and the lease predicate mirrors the pod predicate exactly, so it can never close funded or foreign capacity. The residual over-eviction of containers (killing more ranks than the swap needs) is inherent to the coarse group="0" pod identity and cannot be fixed without R28b's per-slot pod identity.

After applying the fix (close every open lease of the victim whose leaseGroupIndex == group, right after removePodsForGroups), the same test passes and `go test ./controllers/` stays green.

It does NOT fix the container over-eviction (killing ranks on non-spare slots), which is inherent to R28b's group="0" pod identity and needs per-slot pod addressing to resolve.

It then closes ONLY the single triggering lease via CloseLease(lease, ...) at line 1477; the victim's other open leases in the same group are never touched.

Nothing later closes it — the post-loop sweep (line 1343-1351) only closes leases of runs whose tracker phase == RunPhaseFailed, and reclaimSquatter only ever writes Pending, never Failed; cleanupDeletedRun only runs when the Run object is deleted. So the lease holds the slot (marks it occupied, counted by baseGangGPUsForRun) until the Run is deleted.

Option (b) from WHERE TO PUSH — slot-granular pod removal — is genuinely impossible with the current pod model: PodManifest carries NodeName (a bare node string) and a GPUs count, no node#ordinal (binder.go 130-138), and in my fixture both filler pods are NodeName="node-b" with no way to distinguish the node-b#0 pod from the node-b#1 pod. Demonstrates both over-eviction (node-b#1 pod deleted though swap needs only node-b#0) and the orphaned sibling lease.
votes.recommendedFix: Make the two planes agree at the granularity the pod model can actually express. Since removePodsForGroups deletes the victim's ENTIRE group (a gang missing ranks is dead, and PodManifest has no ordinal so nothing finer is possible), reclaimSquatter must close EVERY open lease the victim holds in that group, not just the triggering one. Concretely, in reclaimSquatter after removePodsForGroups, iterate c.State.Leases and CloseLease(other, "ReclaimedBySpare", now) for every open lease with victimKey and leaseGroupIndex==group; then compute baseGangGPUsForRun AFTER that closure so the "still wide enough to stay Running" branch reflects only the victim's OTHER groups (under R28b there are none, so the run correctly requeues from zero). Reaper check: this closes only leases whose containers were just deleted by the same call, so it cannot strand funded work or over-close a live slot — it removes an orphan, it does not create one; and it does not touch Lease.Status outside CloseLease, respecting the sole-closer rule. Caveat / true cost: this only stops the orphan-lease billing. The over-eviction itself (one contended slot nuking the victim's whole gang) is inherent to R28b's coarsened identity and cannot be fixed here — it needs R28b resolved (real per-group labels so a group is a gang, not the whole run) plus a pod model that carries node#ordinal so slot-granular pod removal becomes possible. adjudicated.title: Malleable-above-min victim left Running with open leases whose pods were deleted — a two-plane split invisible to the oracle

votes.reasoning:
F5 is real in the current tree (HEAD e2b5355), reproduced and confirmed by execution.

MECHANISM. reclaimSquatter is called for an unfunded run squatting the swap's exact spare slots (run_controller.go:1297-1303). It closes exactly ONE lease — the squatter — then computes group := leaseGroupIndex(lease) which, for a production-shaped lease (the sole committer stamps no group index), defaults to "0" (leaseGroupIndex, run_controller.go:2128-2135). It then calls removePodsForGroups(victimKey, {"0"}). Because emitCohortPods and emitSparePods hardcode LabelGroupIndex:"0" on EVERY pod (run_controller.go:2070, 1949), that call deletes ALL of the victim's pods, not just the squatter's. If the victim is malleable and still >= minRunnableGPUs after the one lease closes, reclaimSquatter takes the "stays Running" branch (run_controller.go:1487-1494) and returns, leaving the run Running with its OTHER base leases still open but zero pods behind them.

STABILITY. Nothing repairs it. runGangComplete returns false when a run has no pods (sawActive stays false, run_controller.go:508-523), so completeRun never fires. reconcileElasticRun grows/shrinks off lease width and never re-creates base pods. topUpActiveGang — the only pod-restorer — is gated behind phase != Running at BOTH call sites (Reconcile adoption at run_controller.go:208-238, activateReservation at run_controller.go:810-833), and the codebase itself states this at run_controller.go:1702 ("topUpActiveGang is only reachable pre-Running"). There is no settleLeases/ledger-audit sweep in controllers/. I drove Reconcile 20 times over 20 simulated hours: the run stays Running, the leftover lease stays open, and its pod is never recreated.

ORACLE BLINDNESS. snapshotWorld computes RunnableGPUs from runnableGPUsForRun (leases) and sets AwaitingMint := activePods > openActiveLeases (invariants.go:88-93). INV-WIDTH-ASSEMBLED then only fires when width (leases) < min. There is a check for pods>leases (AwaitingMint) but NONE for leases>pods, so a Running run with open leases and zero pods produces no violation — my reproduction ran the deferred checkInvariants and did not panic.

ROOT CAUSE / PROVENANCE. This is a manifestation of R28b (all pods collapse to group "0" because real per-group identity never reaches the pods or leases). The counterfactual test with distinct group labels shows filler-keep's pod survives — no split. Proximately, 8a1dc2c introduced it: git show 8a1dc2c deletes reclaimSquatter's `if group != ""` guard and switches to leaseGroupIndex (default "0"). Before it, group was "" and removePodsForGroups({""}) matched no "0"-labelled pods, so no pods were deleted (that was the R27 ledger-only-eviction bug). 8a1dc2c fixed the single-lease squatter case but, in doing so, opened the opposite half-plane door for the malleable-above-min case — precisely the "reached by a new door" pattern the playbook warns about. Per AGENTS.md, "rooted in a known bug / pre-existing" is a classification, not a dismissal, and a change that trades one half-plane defect for another keeps the finding on the table.

b4f6a6d's runnableGPUsForRun change is irrelevant here: reclaimSquatter's Running gate still uses baseGangGPUsForRun (run_controller.go:1487); either width notion keeps the run Running when it is above min. e2b5355 (NodeReconciler fencing) does not touch this path.

adjudicated.title: reclaimSquatter widens the blast radius of the pre-existing stale-ev misclassification in HandleNodeFailure pass 2

votes.reasoning:
The finding's mechanical claim holds against HEAD (e2b5355), and I proved the load-bearing lemma by execution rather than argument.

TRACE. HandleNodeFailure computes the funding derivation exactly once, at run_controller.go:1231 (`ev := c.evaluate(now)`), immediately after pass 1 and before the pass-2 loop. The comment above it (1228-1231) frames this as "computed once ... the only way to know" — convenience, not a justified staleness invariant. That single `ev` is the ONLY funding evaluation inside the function (grep confirms: no re-evaluate between 1182 and 1361). Pass 2 then consults it at run_controller.go:1297 (`class, classified := ev.Class(other)`) for every exact-slot conflict, and reclaims when it reads Unfunded (1298 -> 1303 `c.reclaimSquatter(...)`). Meanwhile pass 2 CLOSES leases as it runs: 1247 (orphan), 1283 (own stale), 1321/1322 (the swap: a funded active AND a funded spare), and reclaimSquatter itself at 1477. The swap closures at 1321/1322 execute at the END of iteration i, so iteration i+1's ev.Class(other) at 1297 is consulted AFTER real closures the snapshot never saw. `ev.Class` (funding/evaluate.go:1096-1099) is a pure lookup into `classes map[string]Class // by LeaseKey, open leases only` (evaluate.go:69), a frozen map built from the open-lease set at 1231. So a later `other` is classified against a world that no longer exists — exactly as the finding states.

DIRECTION (the reviewer's core question). Only direction (a), evict-funded, is possible; (b) is impossible. Proof: pass 2 never MINTS a lease — emitSwapPod emits a pod and the plugin mints at PreBind, outside this call — it only closes. So the real open-lease set is a strict subset of ev's. Every funding mechanism (envelope width/concurrency, GPU-hour integrals at Now, lending headroom, aggregate caps) is monotone: removing an open lease weakly RAISES every other lease's funded standing and never lowers it. Therefore stale ev can only OVER-report Unfunded, never under-report it. Consequence: the engine may reclaim a squatter that stale-ev calls Unfunded but that the current world funds (a); it can never fail to reclaim a genuinely-Unfunded squatter (b). So this is the data-loss direction, not a missed optimization.

EXECUTED LEMMA. I built pkg/funding/f6_stale_test.go in a temp copy: one envelope, concurrency cap 8; run A (older) holds lA width 8, run B (younger) squats lB width 4. With both open, Evaluate classes lB Unfunded (over the cap). Close lA, re-Evaluate: lB flips to Owned. Output: "lB flips Unfunded -> Owned when lA closes." This is the promotion the stale snapshot misses. Because reclaimSquatter now evicts in BOTH planes — CloseLease at 1477 plus c.removePodsForGroups at 1485 (kills the container) plus a phase demote — a stale-ev false positive now DESTROYS a workload that is funded in the current world, where the pre-R27 ledger-only closeLease merely mis-stated the ledger. That is precisely the finding's title: reclaimSquatter widened the blast radius of the pre-existing stale-ev misclassification. And it violates the function's own declared contract at 1286-1296: "evicting it belongs to the resolver, which ranks by class — not to this function." A stale ev makes HandleNodeFailure usurp exactly the funded-vs-funded eviction it says it must not make.

REACHABILITY. Requires two active leases on the same failed node from runs sharing a budget context (envelope width cap, family excess, or lending pool), where the first's teardown frees the capacity that funds a squatter examined later. Multiple tenants oversubscribing one node and node failures are routine over months; this is not exotic. Not dismissible under "unusual sequence."

WHY NOT the naive fix (checked against the reaper question). Recomputing ev before each ev.Class would reflect the current world but make the reclaim decision depend on the order c.State.Leases stores the failing leases — different orders close different funded leases first, flipping later classifications. That is playbook class 3 (last-writer-wins), the reaper this file fights. The single pre-pass evaluation is at least order-INDEPENDENT; the fix must preserve that.
votes.reproduction: Executed pkg/funding/f6_stale_test.go (TestF6_ClosingLeaseFlipsClass) in a temp copy of the repo. Setup: budget owner "team", one envelope "west" with concurrency cap 8; run A (created at base) holds lease lA width 8, run B (created base+30m) holds lease lB width 4, all in envelope west. Result: `go test ./pkg/funding -run TestF6_ClosingLeaseFlipsClass -v` PASSES with logs: "stale ev (both open): lB = Unfunded" and "fresh ev (lA closed): lB = Owned". This demonstrates that closing one open lease promotes another open lease's derived class, so the single ev computed at run_controller.go:1231 misclassifies any squatter whose funding improved due to closures made earlier in the pass-2 sweep (e.g. the Swap closures at 1321-1322). I did not build the full ClusterState controller scenario; the funding lemma plus the code trace (single ev at 1231, reused at 1297 after intervening closures, monotone-toward-funded because pass 2 only closes leases) establishes both the mechanism and its single direction.
votes.recommendedFix: Do NOT recompute ev per iteration — that reintroduces order-dependence (class 3), since which funded lease closes first would change later classifications. Instead make the reclaim classification consistent with the world it acts on, order-independently. Two acceptable shapes: (1) Defer reclamation: during pass 2, collect candidate squatter leases (those on a spare's exact slots that stale-ev calls Unfunded) without evicting; after the pass-2 loop has finished all its closures, call c.evaluate(now) ONCE more and reclaim only the candidates still Unfunded in that final world. One post-loop evaluation is both current-world-accurate and order-independent. This changes semantics — a squatter that becomes funded purely because the failing run's own teardown freed capacity gets reprieved — so record the decision in quota-semantics.md. (2) If, conversely, the intent is deliberately to judge reclaim-eligibility at the failure instant (pre-teardown) so a squatter is not reprieved by transient teardown capacity, then keep the single ev but replace the "computed once ... only way to know" comment at 1228 with an explicit statement that reclaim eligibility is fixed at the failure instant and why (order-independence + no reprieve), turning an accidental snapshot into a chosen invariant. Either way the reaper check passes: neither option lets lease slice order determine who is evicted. Given severity is low (the victim is opportunistic unfunded-class work that demotes to Pending and re-admits), option (2) with a corrected comment is the cheapest correct resolution if the design agrees a same-failure reprieve is undesirable.

Because pass 2 only ever CLOSES leases (minting happens later, at PreBind, by the plugin), the open-lease set is monotonically shrinking, so funding can only move up: the stale ev can only OVER-report Unfunded, never under-report. Therefore the error is exclusively direction (a): reclaimSquatter, gated on stale Unfunded, EVICTS a run that is FUNDED in the true post-closure world. Direction (b) (declining a truly-unfunded squatter) is unreachable.

The staleness that bites comes from the FUNDED swap/failGroup closures, which predate reclaimSquatter. But the finding's TITLE — "widens the blast radius" — is exactly right: `git show 7a681c0` shows the Unfunded branch changed from `closeLease(other, "ReclaimedBySpare", now)` (a ledger-only close) to `c.reclaimSquatter(other, otherKey, now)`, which additionally removePodsForGroups (kills the victim's running container) and demotes its phase to Pending. So a stale-ev misclassification that was previously a bounded ledger error now KILLS a funded run's in-flight GPU work and requeues it. reclaimSquatter is live in production post-8a1dc2c (leaseGroupIndex defaults the missing label to "0" and the pods are labelled "0"), so this is not a dead-code fix like F3.

A funded run C, sharing an envelope with a run whose funded lease died with the node and was closed in an earlier pass-2 iteration, and squatting the spare's exact slots (an oversubscription the engine explicitly tolerates, invariant.go), is classified Unfunded by the stale ev though it is Owned/Shared/Borrowed in the true post-closure world. reclaimSquatter closes its lease, removes its pods (its running containers die → lost progress), and demotes it to Pending. No budget is charged forever (this is not an immortal-lease bug); the harm is DATA LOSS of a funded run plus a requeue. Narrow geometry (multi-lease node failure + shared envelope + cross-run exact-slot squatter + budget tight enough to have made C Unfunded-at-entry), which is why low severity is right; but each ingredient is legal and node failures + budget rollovers run for months.

Computing ev once against the fixed post-pass-1 world makes every reclaim decision use ONE order-free baseline; that order-independence is deliberate (the phases tracker, order_independence_test.go, history-run-phase-writers.md, "seventh defect of this exact class"). A fresh ev mid-loop would classify each `other` against whatever earlier iterations happened to close, i.e. against c.State.Leases order — reintroducing the exact order-dependent-phase defect class this path has shipped repeatedly. So per-iteration re-eval trades a narrow data-loss bug for a broad order-dependence bug.
votes.reproduction: Executed: pkg/funding/f6_monotone_test.go in a scratch copy of the repo. The full end-to-end HandleNodeFailure geometry (two funded actives failing on one node, a cross-run exact-slot squatter on the second group's spare slots, squatter sharing the first group's envelope) is legal per the tolerated-oversubscription invariant but was not built end-to-end; the funding monotonicity that is the sole uncertain link is executed above, and the control-flow links (ev computed once at 1231, read at 1297, funded closures at 1321-1322 across iterations, no re-eval 1234-1336) are read directly.
votes.recommendedFix: Keep a SINGLE evaluation (do not re-evaluate per iteration — that reaps order-independence). Make that one evaluation reflect the world the node failure produces, which is order-free: at line 1231 evaluate against the lease set with every lease on the failed node excluded (they are all going to close regardless of order — `leaseContainsNode(lease, nodeName)`). Removing leases only promotes funding, so gating reclaim on this post-casualty ev can only move `other` from Unfunded toward funded, i.e. it can only UNDER-reclaim (a missed optimization — the safe direction) and never evict funded work. The residual second-order effect (a declined-swap spare release freeing yet more budget) is bounded and also only frees budget, so it too can only cause safe under-reclaim; if you want it exact, exclude releasable spares as well, but that reintroduces circularity with the swap decision and is not worth it for a low finding. adjudicated.title: Terminal branch closes a failed run's out-of-scope spare and surviving-group leases but leaves their pods running (half-plane double-allocation)

votes.reasoning:
F7 is a real, present defect at HEAD (e2b5355), confirmed by execution in a clean-room clone, not by argument. The engine's terminal-failure paths release a run's leases (freeing the GPUs in the ledger) but do NOT remove the run's surviving pods from state.Pods, and Bridge.apply deletes only pods that are ABSENT from state.Pods (controllers/kube/bridge.go:262). So the containers keep running and holding GPUs while funding.Evaluate counts those GPUs as free — the half-plane double-allocation the finding describes.

I reproduced it three ways on pristine HEAD: (1) HandleNodeFailure's post-loop Failed sweep — a 2-group run loses group-0's node with no spare, goes Failed, closeRunLeases closes group-1's lease on the HEALTHY node, yet run-1's pod stays in state.Pods (test logged: phase=Failed, open leases=0, pods=[run-0-Active run-1-Active]); (2) failRun leaving a spare pod on a healthy node; (3) by inspection, applyResolution's resolver-cut branch (run_controller.go ~1746) closes leases with no pod removal — its own comment admits a run's spare can lie outside reservation scope, matching F7's "out-of-scope spare" wording.

completeRun is the proof this is a bug, not a legal state: on the identical shape it closes leases AND removes pods (c.State.Pods = kept, line 548). reclaimSquatter documents that a lease and a pod are two different claims on one GPU and evicts BOTH planes — so the author already treats a ledger-only release as double-allocation everywhere except the terminal paths.

On the FIX and the reaper question: the correct fix is a removeAllRunPods(run) helper mirroring completeRun, called at all three terminal-close sites (failRun, the HandleNodeFailure Failed sweep, the applyResolution resolver-Failed branch). The trap to avoid is the opposite one (F3): a test that uses distinct group labels to demonstrate the half-plane is richer than reality and proves nothing about production while R28b stands.

repro.analysis:
CONFIRMED. F4 is a real defect at the finding's target commit e2b5355 (note: the worktree's `main` is at 6090665, which predates the R27 branch; e2b5355 lives on fix/r27-invariant-oracle, a descendant of main, and is where reclaimSquatter/leaseGroupIndex exist — I exported that exact tree with `git archive` and ran against it).

The two-plane mismatch is exact. Conflict detection is SLOT-granular: HandleNodeFailure pass 2 only visits an `other` lease when `!leaseOccupiesSlots(other, spareSlots)` is false (run_controller.go:1277) — i.e. it holds one of the spare's exact node#ordinal slots. But eviction is GROUP-granular: for an Unfunded conflict it calls `c.reclaimSquatter(other, otherKey, now, phases)` (1303), which computes `group := leaseGroupIndex(lease)` (1476), closes ONLY that one lease with `CloseLease(lease, "ReclaimedBySpare", now)` (1477), then `c.removePodsForGroups(victimKey, map[string]struct{}{group: {}})` (1485). removePodsForGroups deletes every pod whose LabelGroupIndex matches (2646-2648). Under the two production facts given, every lease has no group index → leaseGroupIndex defaults to "0", and every pod is hardcoded group "0", so "the group" is "the whole run": one contended slot deletes the victim's entire pod set cluster-wide, while only the one triggering lease is closed. Any sibling lease on a different slot is left OPEN with its container deleted — an immortal lease (class 9 half-plane / class 1 immortal-lease). My fixture gives the victim two production-shaped active leases (filler-a on the spare's slot node-b#0, filler-b on node-b#2 which the swap never needs). After the swap: filler-a closed, filler-b OPEN, all filler pods gone. It persists across 20 reconciles / 20 simulated hours. The width invariant does not catch it because filler-b counts as 1 open non-spare GPU >= min, so the run legally reports Running while holding a lease over a dead container — exactly the blind spot invariant.go documents (no lease-vs-pod invariant; no same-slot invariant).

FIX ANALYSIS (step 5 — the legal state each proposed fix destroys). Fixes 1 & 2 ("after removePodsForGroups, close every open victim lease whose leaseGroupIndex == group") REAP a legal state. Because funding class is derived per-lease and concurrency-ranked, a single run legally holds a MIX of Owned and Unfunded leases (a malleable run that grew one rank beyond its owned concurrency onto opportunistic capacity — quota-semantics R14, and invariant.go's RunnableGPUs doc explicitly blesses "a run can legally be Running with zero base-gang GPUs"). I built that reachable state: with a Concurrency=1 budget, filler-squat (on the spare slot) derives Unfunded and triggers the reclaim, while its sibling filler-owned (node-b#2) derives Owned. Under R28b both are group "0", so "close every lease in the group" closes filler-owned — the run's funded, running rank. Deciding to evict funded work is the resolver's job; the same function already DECLINES the swap for cross-run funded conflicts (1297-1309) precisely to avoid this. Fixes 1&2 would silently evict funded work of the SAME run — a class-5 identity-coarsening reaper. Fix 3 (node-scope the pod deletion; route dead fixed-width gangs through the terminal sweep; do NOT close sibling leases) avoids the funded-lease reaper but does NOT eliminate F4: filler-squat (node-b#0) and filler-owned (node-b#2) share node-b, and PodManifest carries only NodeName, so node-scoped deletion still deletes filler-owned's pod, and leaving its lease open (correct for a still-Running rank) re-creates the same half-plane orphan for same-node co-ranks — fix 3's own caveat, which my repro demonstrates is load-bearing. Bottom line: F4 is real; all three proposed fixes are partial. A complete fix requires R28b's per-group/per-slot identity so a group is a gang (not the whole run) and pod removal can be slot-granular.
repro.minimalTrigger: A node-failure swap onto a held spare whose exact slots are squatted by an UNFUNDED lease, where the victim run also holds a second (sibling) lease on a different slot. Because every production lease defaults to group "0" and every pod is labeled group "0" (R28b), reclaimSquatter deletes ALL the victim's pods but closes only the one squatting lease. Fixture: funded run "run" active on node-a#0 (failing) + spare on node-b#0; unfunded "filler" with active leases on node-b#0 (spare slot) and node-b#2 (sibling); pods for both filler ranks. Call HandleNodeFailure("node-a"). filler-b ends OPEN with all filler pods deleted.
repro.recommendedFix: Make the lease plane and pod plane agree at the granularity the model can express, without over-reaching into funded work. Because removePodsForGroups deletes the victim's entire group and (under R28b) the group is the whole run, reclaimSquatter must close only the leases whose containers it actually deleted AND must not close funded ones — but funded eviction is the resolver's call, so the honest fix is: scope BOTH planes to the reclaimed spare's slots. This is blocked today because PodManifest carries NodeName/GPUs but no ordinal, so same-node co-ranks (node-b#0 vs node-b#2) cannot be separated. Therefore the correct fix requires R28b (docs/project/remediation/R28-group-identity.md): give leases and pods real per-group/per-slot identity so a group is a gang, not the whole run, and pod removal can be slot-granular. Until then, the minimum consistency fix is: in reclaimSquatter, after removePodsForGroups, close every OPEN victim lease in that group WHOSE DERIVED CLASS IS Unfunded (reuse the ev.Class check already done at the call site), reason "ReclaimedBySpare" — never a bare group match, which reaps Owned/Shared siblings. Any funded sibling on a deleted slot must instead route the victim through the resolver/terminal path, not be silently closed here. Add a test that asserts on BOTH state.Leases and state.Pods for a victim holding a sibling lease AND a mixed Owned/Unfunded victim.

reproductions.title: Malleable-above-min victim left Running with open leases whose pods were deleted — a two-plane split invisible to the oracle

repro.observedOutput:
Against a tree extracted from commit e2b5355 (`git archive e2b5355`), with the invariant oracle wired into every entry point and PANICKING under `go test`:

--- PASS: TestF5_MalleableAboveMinVictimLeftRunningWithOpenLeasesNoPods

=== RUN   TestF5_CleanImmortalLease   (desired==post-reclaim width, so no elastic grow)

=== RUN   TestF5_Counterfactual_DistinctGroupsSpareTheSurvivor
  with distinct groups: filler total pods=2, survivor(group1) pods=2
  MECHANISM CONFIRMED: distinct per-slice identity leaves filler-keep's containers alive; the F5 split exists ONLY because leaseGroupIndex and every pod collapse to "0".
--- PASS: TestF5_Counterfactual_DistinctGroupsSpareTheSurvivor
ok  github.com/davidlangworthy/jobtree/controllers
repro.analysis:
F5 REPRODUCED and CONFIRMED against e2b5355, with the oracle active (a deferred checkInvariants that panics under test). The panel's still-present verdict holds.

MECHANISM (verified by execution, not argument). A funded run's node-failure swap needs the exact spare slots (node-b#0,#1) that an UNFUNDED malleable run ("filler") squats on. In HandleNodeFailure's pass-2 exact-slot loop the squatter derives funding.ClassUnfunded, so reclaimSquatter runs. Inside reclaimSquatter: `group := leaseGroupIndex(lease)` (run_controller.go:1476) which, for a production-shaped lease (admission.PodLeaseWithRole stamps NO LabelGroupIndex), defaults to "0" (leaseGroupIndex, :2134 `return "0"`); then `CloseLease(lease,...)` closes exactly ONE lease (:1477); then `c.removePodsForGroups(victimKey, map[string]struct{}{group: {}})` (:1485). Because emitCohortPods/emitSparePods hardcode LabelGroupIndex "0" on EVERY pod, removePodsForGroups keyed on "0" deletes ALL of the victim's pods — the surviving filler-keep group's containers included — while only one lease was closed. The gate `if baseGangGPUsForRun(victimKey, c.State.Leases) >= minRunnableGPUs(victim)` (:1487) is 2>=2 (malleable min=2; filler-keep still open = 2 base-gang GPUs), so the run is left RUNNING. Result: a Running run holding an open active lease (filler-keep) with zero backing containers — a two-plane split. My counterfactual (distinct group labels: squatter pods "0", survivor pods "1") leaves the survivor's 2 pods intact, isolating the coarse-"0" collapse as the sole cause — i.e. the durable fix is R28b (per-slice identity reaching leases and pods).

ORACLE BLINDNESS (verified). No installed invariant fires. INV-WIDTH-ASSEMBLED derives width from runnableGPUsForRun (open non-spare LEASES = 2), not pods, so 2>=min(2) passes. The AwaitingMint gate is `activePods > openActiveLeases` (invariants.go:93); after the reclaim activePods(0) < openActiveLeases(1), so AwaitingMint is FALSE and the width check is not even skipped — it just passes. INV-TERMINAL-PRESENT only checks terminal runs; the victim is Running. The oracle is structurally blind to leases-exceeding-pods on a Running run. The reverse (pods>leases) is what AwaitingMint watches; F5 is the unchecked direction.

PERSISTENCE (verified over 20 reconciles / 20 simulated hours). filler-keep NEVER closes and the run NEVER leaves Running. runGangComplete requires at least one live active pod to complete (run_controller.go:508-524; sawActive stays false with 0 pods), so the run cannot self-complete; nothing fails it. In the clean variant (DesiredTotalGPUs == post-reclaim width) the elastic reconcile is a pure no-op and the run sits Running with an open pod-less lease and ZERO pods for the full 20h — an immortal lease charging its budget and holding node-c#0,#1 forever. NUANCE worth flagging on the author's own claim "nothing re-creates the pods: topUpActiveGang is only reachable pre-Running": for a malleable run whose Desired>Allocated, reconcileElasticRun DOES emit pods — but via growRun toward Desired on NEW slots, never re-pairing filler-keep with a container and never closing it. In the pure sim (no plugin to mint) those grow pods leak unbounded (2,4,...,40); in production the plugin would mint grow leases, growing the lease/GPU footprint further while filler-keep's slot stays pod-less. Either way the orphaned lease is immortal.

ADVERSARIAL CHECK OF THE PROPOSED FIXES. (1) R28b (thread per-slice identity into leases+pods): destroys no legal state; it only makes removePodsForGroups precise, exactly as the counterfactual proves. Safest and durable. (2) Slot/node-scoped pod removal (removeSparePodOnNodes-style, delete only pods on the reclaimed node): strictly safer than the cluster-wide group-"0" match — in this fixture it deletes filler-squat's node-b pods and spares filler-keep's node-c pods. Pods carry only NodeName (no ordinal), so node-scoping is the finest granularity available pre-R28b, and it destroys no legal state a lease-derived group would not already touch. (3) The proposed BACKSTOP invariant — fire on a Running, !AwaitingMint, KnownToLedger run with activePods < openActiveLeases — is the risky one, and it is a near neighbour of the explicitly REJECTED "a Running run holds at least one open active lease". It survives the two states the author checked (checkpoint-grace parks PENDING with pods>leases, opposite direction; the swap window has a swap pod so pods>=leases and AwaitingMint is true). The LEGAL state it risks reaping that the author did NOT rule out: a gang running to completion where some ranks have Succeeded and their pods have been reaped (kubelet/GC/TTL) while their leases stay OPEN pending whole-gang completion — the engine keeps a run Running until EVERY active pod succeeds (run_controller.go:143-145), and if a finished rank's pod is gone before the run completes, that run legally has openActiveLeases > activePods while Running and not awaiting a mint. In the pure engine a Succeeded pod is retained (runGangComplete iterates it), so I could not force this state in-sim, hence PLAUSIBLE not CONFIRMED — but it is exactly the kind of state that turned a plausible invariant into a reaper before, and it must be excluded against the real pod lifecycle before that backstop ships. Recommendation: fix in reclaimSquatter (R28b, or node-scoped removal until then) rather than rely on the backstop invariant as the primary remedy.
repro.minimalTrigger: An unfunded MALLEABLE run (min < current width) squatting the exact spare slots a node-failure swap needs, with two base-gang groups on different nodes, all pods stamped LabelGroupIndex "0" (as emitCohortPods hardcodes) and leases carrying no group index (as admission.PodLeaseWithRole mints). HandleNodeFailure -> reclaimSquatter closes the one squatting lease, removePodsForGroups({"0"}) deletes ALL victim pods, the width gate (baseGangGPUs >= min) leaves it Running -> Running run with an open lease and zero backing pods, invisible to the oracle, never closed.
repro.recommendedFix: Fix in reclaimSquatter; do not rely on a backstop oracle check as the primary remedy. Durable: land R28b so the packer's per-slice identity reaches both the Lease (LabelGroupIndex) and the pods, so removePodsForGroups({group}) deletes only the reclaimed slot's pod and the survivor lease+pod stay paired (proven by the counterfactual test). Until R28b: replace the cluster-wide group-"0" removal (run_controller.go:1485) with a node/slot-scoped removal (mirror removeSparePodOnNodes on lease.Spec.Slice.Nodes) so only the pod on the reclaimed node is deleted; OR, forced by coarse identity, ALSO close the victim's group-"0" base-gang leases whose pods were just deleted so both planes drop together and the run demotes to Pending and re-admits (R14). Any backstop invariant (Running, !AwaitingMint, KnownToLedger, activePods < openActiveLeases) must first be proven not to reap a Running gang whose Succeeded ranks' pods were reaped while their leases stay open pending whole-gang completion.

Oracle: grep "INVARIANT VIOLATION" over the reproduction run => 0 panics. The reclaimed state is LEGAL, not a corruption.
repro.minimalTrigger: A node fails while it carries (a) an active lease of a run that has a spare on some slots, and (b) an active lease of a DIFFERENT run whose budget's concurrency is also consumed by a third run squatting on those exact spare slots. Concretely: budget "other" has Concurrency=2; run "hog" (older, ranks first) holds 2 GPUs on the failed node-a and consumes all of budget other's concurrency; run "filler" (younger) holds 2 GPUs on node-b#0,#1 — the exact slots of victim's spare — so at the failure instant filler is over-cap and derives Unfunded. victim (budget team) holds an active on node-a and a spare on node-b#0,#1. HandleNodeFailure("node-a") computes `ev` once (run_controller.go:1231, before pass 2), so it sees hog still open and filler Unfunded; pass 2 then reclaims filler via reclaimSquatter (class==ClassUnfunded at 1297-1303) AND separately closes hog (a casualty of the same node). In the world the failure actually produces (hog dead), filler is Owned.
repro.analysis:
CONFIRMED. The finding's mechanical claim holds verbatim against HEAD e2b5355: HandleNodeFailure computes the funding derivation exactly once at run_controller.go:1231 (`ev := c.evaluate(now)`), immediately after pass 1 and before the pass-2 loop, and that single map is the only funding evaluation in the function. Pass 2 then closes leases as it runs — the swap path closes the spare and failed active (1321-1322), failGroupWithoutSpare closes leases (1403,1411), reclaimSquatter closes squatters (1477) — while every reclaim decision reads the never-refreshed `ev` at 1297 (`class, classified := ev.Class(other)`) and reclaims on ClassUnfunded at 1298/1303. Because closing leases only frees budget, a squatter the stale ev calls Unfunded can be funded in the post-closure world. My test drove exactly this: filler is genuinely Unfunded at the failure instant (hog holds budget other's Concurrency=2, verified by the pre-failure `pre := c.evaluate(now)` premise check), gets reclaimed with reason "ReclaimedBySpare" and demoted to Pending, yet in the world the failure produces — hog's lease closed as a casualty of the same node-a failure — filler classifies as Owned (verified by the counterfactual evaluation). So the reclaim widened its blast radius past work that the failure itself funds.

Two important qualifications the reproduction establishes and that keep this at LOW:

1) It is NOT an immortal lease and NOT an oracle violation. reclaimSquatter acts in both planes (closes the lease AND removePodsForGroups, per the R28a/half-plane fixes), filler lands in Pending, and the grep for INVARIANT VIOLATION returns 0 — the produced state is legal and self-correcting (filler re-admits when capacity returns, per quota-semantics R14). The defect is an unnecessary/incorrect eviction driven by a stale classification, not a corruption or leak. The 20-reconcile persistence check for immortality is therefore N/A: there is no leaked lease to persist.

2) Whether this is even a bug is an undocumented policy question, which is why it is genuinely LOW. filler is unfunded AT THE FAILURE INSTANT — hog is, at that instant, a live funded claim. hog dies because of the same failure. So "should filler be reprieved?" is exactly the "same-failure reprieve" question the finding raises, and it is NOT settled in quota-semantics.md.

Adversarial check of the proposed fix (step 5): I mutated in the stronger proposed fix — evaluate `ev` against the lease set with every failed-node lease excluded (the post-casualty world) — and the SAME scenario flipped: filler stays open (Owned in that world), the swap is DECLINED, and crucially **victim phase=Failed**. That is the legal, arguably-desirable state the fix DESTROYS: today's reclaim keeps a genuinely-funded team-budget run (victim) Running through a node failure by reclaiming slots from a squatter whose Owned status is a transient artifact of a co-tenant's death. Reprieving filler makes the funded victim lose its node-failure recovery and fail — and when hog re-admits into budget other, filler goes back over-cap to Unfunded anyway, so the reprieve was to temporary funding. The mutation both proves the reproduction is load-bearing on the staleness (it flips only when ev reflects the post-casualty world) and exposes that the "obvious" fix trades a funded run's fault tolerance for a squatter's transient funding.
repro.recommendedFix: Do NOT adopt the "exclude failed-node leases from ev" reshape as-is: the mutation shows it fails a genuinely-funded victim run (victim phase=Failed) to reprieve a squatter whose Owned status is a transient artifact of the co-tenant's death — and the reprieve evaporates the moment the dead run re-admits. Both proposed re-evaluation shapes change semantics in a direction that is at least as questionable as the current behavior. Given severity is LOW, the reclaim produces a LEGAL, self-correcting state (no immortal lease, no oracle violation, filler re-admits), and the reclaim keeps the funded victim Running, the cheapest correct resolution is finding-option (2): keep the single `ev`, and REPLACE the "computed once ... the only way to know" comment at run_controller.go:1228-1230 with an explicit statement that reclaim eligibility is deliberately fixed at the failure instant (pre-teardown) — for order-independence AND to deny a squatter a reprieve from transient teardown capacity that the failing run's replacement/re-admission will reclaim anyway. Record that decision in quota-semantics.md so it is a chosen invariant, not an accidental snapshot. Only pursue a re-evaluation-based fix if the design explicitly decides a same-failure reprieve is desirable, in which case option (1) (defer reclamation, single post-loop c.evaluate) is order-independent whereas per-iteration re-evaluation reintroduces the class-3 order dependence.

=== RUN   TestF7_SurvivingPodIsImmortalAcrossReconciles
    after 20 reconciles / 20h: run phase=Pending  lease-b closed=true("RunFailed")  pod-b present=true
    F7 PERSISTS: 20h later the surviving pod is STILL in state.Pods while its lease stays closed — an immortal half-plane double-allocation
--- FAIL: TestF7_SurvivingPodIsImmortalAcrossReconciles

=== RUN   TestF7_FailRunLeavesSurvivingPodRunning
    failRun: phase=Failed  lease-b closed=true("RunFailed")  pod-b present=true
    F7 REPRODUCED via failRun: lease b closed (GPU free in ledger) but pod run-pod-b still running on healthy node-b
--- FAIL: TestF7_FailRunLeavesSurvivingPodRunning

No invariant-oracle panic on any repro (pkg/invariant reasons about leases only). After applying the proposed removeAllRunPods fix: all three F7 tests PASS and the entire controllers suite (incl. checkpoint_grace_test) stays green.
repro.analysis:
F7 is REAL, PRESENT at HEAD (e2b5355), and PERSISTENT. I reproduced it by execution in a copy of the tree, using a production-shaped fixture (leases with no LabelGroupIndex per admission.PodLeaseWithRole; pods labelled group "0" per emitCohortPods).

Mechanism, confirmed by reading and executing:
1. controllers/run_controller.go closeRunLeases (~line 1512) closes a run's open leases and NEVER touches c.State.Pods.
2. Three terminal-failure sites drive a run Failed and call closeRunLeases without culling the run's surviving pods: failRun (~708), HandleNodeFailure's post-loop `for runKey, phase := range phases { if phase==RunPhaseFailed ... closeRunLeases }` sweep (~1343-1355), and applyResolution's `default:` branch that sets RunPhaseFailed (~1732-1749). applyResolution DOES cull pods, but only for closedGroups (groups named by a resolver action, ~1676-1688); a surviving group's pod / out-of-scope spare pod has no action and is left behind.
3. controllers/kube/bridge.go apply (line 261-266) deletes only Pods ABSENT from state.Pods. A pod that stays in state.Pods is never deleted, so its container keeps running.

Result: the ledger closes the surviving group's lease (funding.Evaluate stops classifying it → node-b#0 counts as free) while the kubelet keeps the container on node-b#0. That is exactly playbook class 9 HALF-PLANE ACTION in the "pod running, lease closed" direction.

Contrast confirms the defect is a genuine omission: completeRun (~541-548) and cleanupDeletedRun both release BOTH planes. The three failure sites release only the ledger.

Persistence: I drove Reconcile 20 times over 20 simulated hours on pristine HEAD. lease-b stays closed and pod-b stays in state.Pods the whole time — an immortal double-allocation that lives until a human deletes the Run. Notably the run even flipped Failed→Pending during the loop: the engine re-planned the now-"free" node-b while the old container still held it, which is precisely the downstream "engine plans new work onto an occupied GPU" consequence class 9 warns about.

The invariant oracle does NOT catch this: pkg/invariant projects only Runs and Leases, never Pods, so TerminalPresent is satisfied (both leases closed) and no panic fires. F7 is invisible to every existing rail — no test failed, no oracle fired.

Adversarial check of the proposed fix (removeAllRunPods called at the three terminal sites, NOT at failGroupWithoutSpare's grace branch, guarded by CheckpointDeadline==nil at the sweep): I implemented it and it turns all three repros green while the full suite — including checkpoint_grace_test — stays green. The ONE legal state at risk is the checkpoint-grace half-plane (failGroupWithoutSpare parks the run Pending with a CheckpointDeadline and deliberately leaves surviving containers running to write a checkpoint). The fix protects it correctly and by two independent arguments: (a) that branch sets Pending, and the sweep and failRun act only on Failed / post-deadline runs, so the grace window is never reached; (b) the CheckpointDeadline==nil guard is belt-and-suspenders. As long as removeAllRunPods is keyed on the TERMINAL transition and never added to the grace branch, it destroys no legal state. A general "lease closed → drop pod" scan WOULD reap the grace window — the proposers correctly reject that shape.
repro.minimalTrigger: A run spans two nodes with no spare (production shape: leases carry LabelRunName+LabelRunRole and NO group label; pods labelled group "0"). node-a fails via HandleNodeFailure("node-a"): the node-a slice takes the no-spare path (failGroupWithoutSpare → Failed, no checkpoint), and the post-loop Failed sweep closes the SURVIVING node-b lease via closeRunLeases. The node-b pod is never removed from state.Pods, so bridge.apply never deletes it. Same result reaching failRun directly (checkpoint-grace expiry) and applyResolution's terminal branch (surviving-group / out-of-scope-spare pods).
repro.testSource: Wrote controllers/f7_repro_test.go in a throwaway copy of HEAD at /tmp/.../scratchpad/jtree. It reuses the existing production-shaped prodLease helper (resolver_settlement_test.go: LabelRunName+LabelRunRole, no LabelGroupIndex) and adds prodPod (LabelGroupIndex "0", as emitCohortPods hardcodes). Three tests: TestF7_NodeFailureSweepLeavesSurvivingPodRunning (2-node run, no spare, fail node-a → asserts surviving node-b pod absent once its lease is swept), TestF7_SurvivingPodIsImmortalAcrossReconciles (same, then 20× Reconcile over 20h), TestF7_FailRunLeavesSurvivingPodRunning (failRun directly with a surviving-group pod present). All three FAIL on pristine HEAD and PASS after adding removeAllRunPods at the three terminal sites.

reproductions.title: New resolver-settlement tests omit the pod plane and grow leases, so neither the terminal-branch half-plane nor the malleable-grow kill is covered

repro.observedOutput:
Working at e2b5355 (the reviewed HEAD; the worktree's own HEAD 6090665 is a divergent branch that does NOT contain the R27/R28/#36 fixes, so I checked out e2b5355 into a temp copy).

== Existing resolver-settlement suite (baseline, all pass) ==

(No invariant panic in any run — pkg/invariant's World projects only Runs+Leases, never Pods, so a leftover pod is invisible to the oracle.)
repro.analysis:
F9 bundles two coverage gaps in controllers/resolver_settlement_test.go; they resolve differently, exactly as the skeptics said.

(b) The malleable-grow-kill gap is CLOSED. b4f6a6d added TestResolverKeepsAMalleableRunRunningWhenGrowWidthCoversItsMinimum (resolver_settlement_test.go:244-287): it builds a real Grow lease (grow.Spec.Reason = binder.LeaseReasonGrow), cuts the BASE group via applyResolution, and asserts the run stays Running and the grow lease survives. I confirmed it is load-bearing: reverting the width gate at run_controller.go:1719 from runnableGPUsForRun to baseGangGPUsForRun makes it FAIL with its own reaper message. Half of F9 is genuinely fixed.

(a) The terminal-branch pod-plane gap is STILL PRESENT as a coverage gap: none of the four resolver-settlement tests builds any Pods, so none asserts that applyResolution's terminal (RunPhaseFailed) branch settles the pod plane. True structural fact of the file.

But the DEFECT the gap points at is masked in production, and this is the load-bearing conclusion. applyResolution evicts pods in exactly one place: the group-scoped drop at run_controller.go:1676-1688, which drops only pods whose namespace/run::group key is in closedGroups (the groups the resolver actually cut). The terminal branch itself (run_controller.go:1733-1750) closes the run's leases via closeRunLeases but never drops the run's pods run-scoped the way completeRun does (run_controller.go:541-548). So a terminal run can keep a pod of a group that was NOT cut. CASE B reproduces this cleanly: a two-group fixed-width run cut on g0 only goes Failed, both leases close, yet g0's sibling pod victim-g1-0 keeps running on node-b — ledger says the slots are free, the container disagrees. The invariant oracle does not catch it, because pkg/invariant's World carries only Runs and Leases, never Pods (there is deliberately no "terminal run holds no pod" invariant).

The catch: that defect is unreachable in the real system. admission.PodLeaseWithRole stamps no LabelGroupIndex, so the resolver's leaseGroupIndex (resolver.go:271-277) defaults every lease to group "0" and every action carries GroupIndex "0"; emitCohortPods hardcodes every pod to group "0" (R28b). With everything in group "0", the closedGroups drop always matches every pod of a cut run, so the terminal run's pods are already evicted before the terminal branch runs. CASE A — a production-shaped single-group fixture (prodLease with the group label deleted, pod group "0", action.GroupIndex derived exactly as the resolver derives it) — PASSES: the pod is gone. So a production-shaped terminal-branch pod test would pass with no code change and prove nothing; only a richer-than-reality multi-group fixture (CASE B) fails.

Verdict: F9(a) survives as a coverage gap, but it currently hides a defect that R28b makes unreachable. The gap cannot be filled with a load-bearing production-shaped test until R28b (distinct group identity) is fixed. The persistence run is noisy (the multi-group fixture re-admits the run to Pending and re-emits pods) precisely because multi-group is not a state production builds; I do not lean on it beyond showing nothing reaps the leftover pod.
repro.minimalTrigger: applyResolution reaches its terminal branch (run_controller.go:1733) for a run whose cut groups (closedGroups) are a strict subset of the run's groups. The run is set Failed and closeRunLeases sweeps all its leases, but the group-scoped pod drop at run_controller.go:1676-1688 evicts only pods in the cut groups, so pods in the run's uncut groups survive on their nodes. Reachable only when a run has more than one distinct group index — which production never produces (leases carry no LabelGroupIndex -> group "0"; emitCohortPods hardcodes pods to group "0"), so the closedGroups drop already covers every production pod and the defect is masked until R28b is fixed.
repro.recommendedFix:
Do NOT paper this over with a distinct-group fixture presented as a passing regression — that is a richer-than-reality test the playbook (class 8) warns against, and CASE A shows a production-shaped test passes with no code change. The durable ordering is: fix R28b (distinct group identity) FIRST; only then does a terminal-branch pod-plane assertion become meaningful and any code fix become load-bearing.

If a code fix is applied now, prefer a run-scoped pod drop in applyResolution's terminal branch, immediately after closeRunLeases at run_controller.go:1745, mirroring completeRun (run_controller.go:541-548): drop every pod whose Namespace+LabelRunName match the failed run. Adversarial check of that fix: it violates no invariant (there is no pod invariant), and in production it is a strict no-op because it drops exactly the pods the closedGroups path already dropped (all group "0"). The one real objection is consistency, not legality: failRun (run_controller.go:701-713) is the OTHER terminal path and deliberately does NOT drop pods — a checkpoint-grace/follow/upstream failure leaves the run's pods in State.Pods until the Run object is deleted (cleanupDeletedRun, reconcilers.go:107-114). So "mirror completeRun" would make the resolver-terminal path diverge from failRun. Either both terminal paths should run-scope-drop pods or neither; fixing only the resolver branch half-addresses and leaves failRun with the same latent half-plane. That divergence — not any invariant violation — is the legal behaviour the fix perturbs.

Three tests: TestF9_TerminalBranch_ProductionShaped_SingleGroup (production-shaped prodLease + group-"0" pod, PASSES -> no production defect), TestF9_TerminalBranch_MultiGroup (two groups, cut one, FAILS -> surviving pod victim-g1-0), TestF9_TerminalBranch_MultiGroup_Persists (20 reconciles).
